Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Medical Information In Merits Briefing; **Court Reporter Fees Paid**
Off Calendar Aug-18-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. Atty. GREGG MCLEAN, Esq. of Messing Adam & Jasmine LLP (Tel: 415-266-1800) for petitioner Anthony Garibaldi;
Atty. REBECCA LIU, Esq. of San Francisco City Attorney's Office (Tel: 415-554-3870) for respondent Retirement Board of the San Francisco Employees' Retirement System.

HEARING RE: PETITIONER ANTHONY GARIBALDI's Motion To Seal Medical Information In Merits Briefing.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Petitioner's motion to seal medical information in merits briefing is denied in part and granted in part. By his motion, Petitioner seeks to file a redacted version of his merits briefing to redact: "(1) the names of his doctors, (2) his medical limitations, and (3) his doctors' medical conclusions about his work restrictions." (MPAA at 1:21-24.) Petitioner did not file any declaration or other evidence in support of the motion, and the declaration that he filed in support of his prior motion to seal portions of the administrative record and pleadings arguably is insufficient to meet his burden under California Rule of Court 2.550. Nevertheless, in recognition of Petitioner's right to privacy in his medical history, the Court will grant the motion as to the second and third categories. The motion is denied as to the first category (the names of Petitioner's doctors), which Petitioner fails to offer any conceivable basis for sealing.

Court to review proposed order submitted by petitioner.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Portions Of Administrative Record And Pleadings
Off Calendar Mar-18-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
PETITIONER ANTHONY GARIBALDI's Motion To Seal Portions Of Administrative Record And Pleadings.

No appearances. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Petitioner Anthony Garibaldi's motion to seal portions of administrative record and pleadings is granted in part and denied in part. As to the administrative record, there was no need for Petitioner to file the motion to seal, as the record was required to be lodged rather than filed. (See Cal. R. Ct. 3.1140.) However, because Petitioner has filed a redacted version of the administrative record with the court, that version may remain under seal on the court's public docket, as the complete unredacted administrative record has been lodged with the Court for review. The motion is denied as to the referenced pleadings (Exs. A, C, E, G, and I). The court has reviewed the proposed redactions, which relate principally to Petitioner's employment before he became a San Francisco firefighter. Petitioner has not met his burden to show that the requirements of California Rule of Court 2.550(d) are met as to any of the redacted information. The mere fact or nature of that prior employment is neither confidential nor protected by Penal Code section 832.7. (See Long Beach Police Officers Assn. v. City of Long Beach (2014) 59 Cal.4th 59, 71 [names of police officers are not "personnel records" protected by section 832.7]; International Federation of Professional & Technical Engineers, Local 21, AFL-CIO v. Superior Court (2007) 42 Cal.4th 318, 328-340 [holding that the public has a general right to know the names and salaries of public officials and employees].) The parties shall comply with Rule of Court 1.201 by redacting from their merits briefs personal identifiers such as Social Security numbers. If Petitioner seeks to seal other information in those briefs, he must file a separate motion to seal in compliance with Rules 2.550 and 2.551.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To File All Court Records Under Seal, Close All Court Hearings And To Proceed As An Anonymous Petitioner
Off Calendar Aug-12-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Atty. STEVEN KAISER, Esq. of Messing Adam & Jasmine LLP (Tel: 916-551-3321) for petitioner;
Atty. REBECCA LIU, Esq. of San Francisco City Attorney's Office (Tel: 415-554-3870) for Respondent Retirement Board of the San Francisco Employees' Retirement System.

Hearing Re: Motion To File All Court Records Under Seal, Close All Court Hearings And To Proceed As An Anonymous Petitioner.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Petitioner's motion to file all court records under seal, close all court hearings and to proceed as an anonymous petitioner is denied. While petitioner is entitled to protect the privacy of his medical records and other personal information, those interests may be protected by moving to seal appropriate portions of the administrative record and pleadings pursuant to California Rules of Court 2.550 and 2.551 if the showing required by those Rules is met. However, petitioner is not entitled to the vastly overbroad relief he seeks by his motion.

Petitioner has not shown that he should be allowed to proceed anonymously. While there is no blanket rule against the use of fictitious names by plaintiffs in cases where (as here) such a procedure is not explicitly authorized by statute, a plaintiff may not use a fictitious name except when unusual circumstances justify protecting his true identity, such as when identification creates a risk of retaliatory physical or mental harm, when anonymity is necessary to preserve the plaintiff's privacy in a matter of sensitive and highly personal nature, or when the anonymous party is compelled to admit his intention to engage in illegal conduct, thereby risking criminal prosecution. (Doe v. Lincoln Unified School Dist. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 758, 767, citing Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir. 2000) 214 F.3d 1058, 1067 [dicta]; see, e.g., Doe v. Superior Court (Luster) (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 750, 754 [rape victim permitted to file complaint under fictitious name in order to preserve her anonymity].) Here, Petitioner's three-paragraph declaration does not show that the disclosure of his identity would jeopardize his physical safety, subject him to retaliation, or otherwise cause an unwarranted invasion of privacy that would outweigh the public right of access to court records.

Petitioner also fails to make a sufficient showing that "all court records" should be filed under seal. Due to the important constitutional principles at stake in open proceedings in all civil cases, the proponent of a sealing order must make a "specific showing of serious injury" that it would suffer if the documents are made available to the public. (Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 100 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1282). "In delineating the injury to be prevented, specificity is essential. Broad allegations of harm, bereft of specific examples or articulated reasoning, are insufficient." (Id.) Petitioner's showing fails to meet this standard.

Finally, California's long-standing open court statute (Code of Civil Procedure section 124 ["the sittings of every court shall be public"] and the First Amendment right of access to trial proceedings preclude Petitioner's request to close all court hearings. (See, e.g., NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV) v. Superior Court (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR#5041, email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com (302/EPS)
